A [general election](/wiki/United_Kingdom_general_elections_overview "United Kingdom general elections overview") was held in the United Kingdom on Thursday, 8 October 1959. The [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") under the leadership of incumbent prime minister [Harold Macmillan](/wiki/Harold_Macmillan "Harold Macmillan") won a landslide victory with a majority of 100 seats. This was their third election victory in a row.

The Conservatives won [the largest number of votes in Scotland](/wiki/1959_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_Scotland "1959 United Kingdom general election in Scotland"), but narrowly failed to win the most seats in that country. They have not made either achievement ever since. Both [Jeremy Thorpe](/wiki/Jeremy_Thorpe "Jeremy Thorpe"), a future Liberal leader, and [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher"), a future Conservative leader and eventually Prime Minister, first entered the House of Commons following this election.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1959_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Following the [Suez Crisis](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis") in 1956, [Anthony Eden](/wiki/Anthony_Eden "Anthony Eden"), the [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") Prime Minister, became unpopular. He resigned early in 1957, and was succeeded by [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer") [Harold Macmillan](/wiki/Harold_Macmillan "Harold Macmillan"). At that point, the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), whose leader [Hugh Gaitskell](/wiki/Hugh_Gaitskell "Hugh Gaitskell") had succeeded [Clement Attlee](/wiki/Clement_Attlee "Clement Attlee") following the [1955 general election](/wiki/1955_United_Kingdom_general_election "1955 United Kingdom general election"), enjoyed large leads in opinion polls over the Conservative Party, and it looked as if Labour would win.[[1]](#cite_note-BBC1959-2)

The [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") also had a new leader, [Jo Grimond](/wiki/Jo_Grimond "Jo Grimond"), so all three parties contested the election with a new leader at the helm.[[1]](#cite_note-BBC1959-2)

However, the Conservatives enjoyed an upturn in fortunes as the economy quickly recovered from the [Recession of 1958](/wiki/Recession_of_1958 "Recession of 1958") under Macmillan's leadership, and his personal approval ratings remained high. At the same time, the Labour Party's popularity suffered due to the rise of industrial disputes in the 1950s and controversies over the [Campaign for Nuclear Disarmament](/wiki/Campaign_for_Nuclear_Disarmament "Campaign for Nuclear Disarmament"). By September 1958, the Conservatives had moved ahead of Labour in the opinion polls.[[1]](#cite_note-BBC1959-2) Parliament was dissolved on 18 September 1959.[[2]](#cite_note-3)

## Campaign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1959_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Campaign")]

All the three main parties had changed leadership since the previous election. The Conservatives fought under the slogan "Life is better with the Conservatives, don't let Labour ruin it" and were boosted by a pre-election economic boom. Macmillan very effectively "summed up" the mood of the British public when he said that most of the people had "never had it so good". Macmillan was very popular, and was described as a politician of the centre ground; in the 1930s he had represented a constituency in [northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England") ([Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Stockton-on-Tees (UK Parliament constituency)")), which had experienced large-scale [unemployment and poverty](/wiki/Interwar_unemployment_and_poverty_in_the_United_Kingdom "Interwar unemployment and poverty in the United Kingdom") during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_the_United_Kingdom "Great Depression in the United Kingdom"). The first week of polling put the Conservatives ahead of Labour by over 5%, but this narrowed as the campaign continued. The Labour Party fought a generally effective campaign, with television broadcasts masterminded by [Tony Benn](/wiki/Tony_Benn "Tony Benn") under the umbrella of their manifesto entitled *Britain Belongs to You*, which accused the Conservatives of complacency over the [growing gap between rich and poor](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality").[[3]](#cite_note-4) Labour's manifesto pledged to reverse reductions in [welfare benefits](/wiki/Welfare_state_in_the_United_Kingdom "Welfare state in the United Kingdom"), [pensions](/wiki/Pensions_in_the_United_Kingdom "Pensions in the United Kingdom"), and [National Health Service](/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service") expenditure; renationalize the steel industry and road haulage; reform secondary education; expand [consumer protections](/wiki/Consumer_protections "Consumer protections"); and create the [Welsh Office](/wiki/Welsh_Office "Welsh Office"). It notably promised not to fully nationalise industries which were performing efficiently and profitably, pivoting away from its earlier emphasis on [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") towards [welfare capitalism](/wiki/Welfare_capitalism "Welfare capitalism").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-5) Hugh Gaitskell made a mistake in declaring that a Labour government would not raise [taxes](/wiki/Taxation_in_the_United_Kingdom "Taxation in the United Kingdom") if it came to power—even though the Labour manifesto contained pledges to increase spending; especially to increase [pensions](/wiki/Pensions_in_the_United_Kingdom "Pensions in the United Kingdom"). Although Gaitskell argued revenue would be provided by economic growth, this led some voters to doubt Labour's spending plans, and is cited as a key reason for their defeat.[[1]](#cite_note-BBC1959-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:0-5)

## Results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1959_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Results")]

Early on during election night, it became clear that the Conservative Party had been returned to government with an increased majority, performing better than it had been expected to. For the fourth general election in a row, the Conservatives increased their number of seats, despite experiencing a slight decrease in their share of the vote. However, there were swings to Labour in parts of [north-west England](/wiki/North_West_England "North West England"), and in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"); where [Scottish Labour](/wiki/Scottish_Labour "Scottish Labour") had overtaken the Conservative-aligned [Unionist Party](/wiki/Unionist_Party_(Scotland) "Unionist Party (Scotland)") as the largest single party in terms of seats, despite winning a slightly smaller share of the vote, thanks to overturning narrow majorities in several constituencies. Future Prime Minister [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") was elected to the House of Commons for the first time as the MP for [Finchley](/wiki/Finchley_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Finchley (UK Parliament constituency)"), where she would represent until her retirement from politics 33 years later at the [1992 general election](/wiki/1992_UK_general_election "1992 UK general election").

For Labour, the result was disappointing; despite appearing more united than they had in recent years under Gaitskell's leadership, the party suffered a third consecutive defeat. [James Callaghan](/wiki/James_Callaghan "James Callaghan") believed that the Conservatives increased their majority in part because working-class Labour voters were still angry at the party for opposing the [Suez conflict](/wiki/Suez_conflict "Suez conflict").[[5]](#cite_note-6) Many of both the Labour Party's supporters and opponents, including Prime Minister Macmillan himself, also blamed the [Gaitskellite](/wiki/Gaitskellite "Gaitskellite") leadership for spending more time preparing to form a government with the assumption that they would win the election than actually campaigning or offering criticism of the Conservative Party's leadership. Political scientists [Mark Abrams](/wiki/Mark_Abrams "Mark Abrams") and [Richard Rose](/wiki/Richard_Rose_(political_scientist) "Richard Rose (political scientist)") blamed Labour's electoral losses from 1959 onwards on an "[embourgeoisement](/wiki/Embourgeoisement "Embourgeoisement")" in which British voters identified increasingly with the middle class, leaving Labour's appeals to the working class less effective. Another key factor was the decline of support of younger voters after 1955, although older voters over the age of 65 increased support for the party in 1959 because of its pledges to expand pensions.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-5)

While the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") earned more than twice as many votes compared to the previous general election, this was largely the result of them nominating nearly double the number of candidates that they did four years prior; their average number of votes-per-candidate only slightly improved. Future Liberal Party Leader [Jeremy Thorpe](/wiki/Jeremy_Thorpe "Jeremy Thorpe") was elected to Parliament for the first time, as the MP for [North Devon](/wiki/North_Devon_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "North Devon (UK Parliament constituency)").

The *[Daily Mirror](/wiki/Daily_Mirror "Daily Mirror")*, despite being a staunch supporter of the Labour Party, wished Macmillan "good luck" on its front page following his election victory.

The [BBC Television Service](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One")'s election coverage, presented by [Richard Dimbleby](/wiki/Richard_Dimbleby "Richard Dimbleby"), was shown on [BBC Parliament](/wiki/BBC_Parliament "BBC Parliament") on 9 October 2009 to mark the fiftieth anniversary of the election and again on 9 October 2019 to mark the sixtieth anniversary.

The 1959 general election was the first election to be covered by [commercial television](/wiki/Commercial_television "Commercial television") in the United Kingdom. The [ITV network](/wiki/ITV_network "ITV network") provided election night coverage from the studios of [Independent Television News](/wiki/Independent_Television_News "Independent Television News") (ITN) in London, with ITV given permission by the [Independent Television Authority](/wiki/Independent_Television_Authority "Independent Television Authority") to use all of the ITV companies on air in 1959 for election links to the main studio in London. [Ian Trethowan](/wiki/Ian_Trethowan "Ian Trethowan") was the presenter for the ITV coverage.[[6]](#cite_note-7)
